web_chunks.text: 4 Definition of Basic and Supplementary Unit of S.I. 1. Metre (m): The metre is the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of 1/299 792 458 of a second. 2. Kilogram (Kg) : The kilogram is the mass of the platinum-iridium prototype which was approved by the ConférenceGénérale des Poids et Mesures, held in Paris in 1889, and kept by the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures. 3. Second (s): The second is the duration of 9192631770 periods of the radiation corresponding to the transition between two hyperfine levels of the ground state of Cesium- 133 atom. 4. Ampere (A) : The ampere is the intensity of a constant current which, if maintained in two straight parallel conductors of infinite length, of negligible circular cross-section, and placed 1 metre apart in vacuum, would produce between these conductors a force equal to 2  10- 7Newton per metre of length. 5. Kelvin (K): Kelvin is the fraction 1/273.16 of the thermodynamic temperature of the triple point of water. 6. Candela (Cd): The candela is the luminous intensity, in a given direction, of a source that emits monochromatic radiation of frequency 540 x 1012 hertz and that has a radiant intensity in that direction of 1/683 watt per steradian. 7. Mole (mol): The mole is the amount of substance of a system which contains as many elementary entities as there are atoms in 0.012 kilogram of Carbon-12. Supplementary units: 1. Radian (rad): It is supplementary unit of plane angle. It is the plane angle subtended at the centre of a circle by an arc of the circle equal to the radius of the circle. It is denoted by 𝜃. 𝜃 = l / r; 𝑙 is length of the arcand 𝑟 is radius of the circle 2. Steradian (Sr): It is supplementary unit of solid angle. It is the angle subtended at the centre of a sphere by a surface area of the sphere having magnitude equal to the square of the radius of the sphere. It is denoted by Ω. Ω = ∆s / r2 SOME IMPORTANT ABBREVIATIONS Symbol Prefix Multiplier Symbol Prefix Multiplier D c m µ n Deci centi milli micro nano 10-1 10-2 10-3 10-6 10-9 da h k M G deca hecto kilo mega giga 101 102 103 106 109

The dimension of c is (A) LT-2 (B) T-3 (C) LT-3 (D) T-3 Short Answer Questions 1. Define Physics. 2. What do you mean by physical quantity? 3. Differentiate between fundamental and derived unit. 4. Write full form of the following system of unit (i) CGS (ii) FPS (iii) MKS 5. Write definition of Dimensions. 6. What is the suitable unit for measuring distance between sun and earth? 7. Write the dimensional formula of the following physical quantity - (i) Momentum (ii) Power (iii) Surface Tension (iv) Strain 8. What is the principle of Homogeneity of Dimensions? 9. Write the S.I & C.G.S units of the following physical quantities- (a) Force (b) Work 10. What are the uses of dimensions? Long Answer Questions 1. Check the correctness of the relation 𝜆 = h /mv; where𝜆is wavelength, h- Planck’s constant, m is mass of the particle and v - velocity of the particle. 2. Explain different types of system of units. 3. Check the correctness of the following relation by using method of dimensions (i) v = u + at (ii) F = mv / r2 (iii) v2 – u2 = 2as 4. What are the limitations of Dimensional analysis? 5. Convert an acceleration of 100 m/s2 into km/hr2. Answers to multiple choice questions: 1 (C) 2 (C) 3 (B) 4 (A) 5 (C)

context:
[Source 1: add/PHYSICS-LEARNING-MATERIAL]
in which numerical value of physical quantity is given and the other as second system

[Source 2: add/PHYSICS-LEARNING-MATERIAL]
4 Definition of Basic and Supplementary Unit of S.I. Metre (m): The metre is the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of 1/299 792 458 of a second. Kilogram (Kg) : The kilogram is the mass of the platinum-iridium prototype which was approved by the ConférenceGénérale des Poids et Mesures, held in Paris in 1889, and kept by the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures. Second (s): The second is the duration of 9192631770 periods of the radiation corresponding to the transition between two hyperfine levels of the ground state of Cesium- 133 atom. Ampere (A) : The ampere is the intensity of a constant current which, if maintained in two straight parallel conductors of infinite length, of negligible circular cross-section, and placed 1 metre apart in vacuum, would produce between these conductors a force equal to 2  10- 7Newton per metre of length. Kelvin (K): Kelvin is the fraction 1/273.16 of the thermodynamic temperature of the triple point of water.

[Source 3: add/PHYSICS-LEARNING-MATERIAL]
What do you mean by physical quantity? Differentiate between fundamental and derived unit. Write full form of the following system of unit (i) CGS (ii) FPS (iii) MKS 5. Write definition of Dimensions. What is the suitable unit for measuring distance between sun and earth? Write the dimensional formula of the following physical quantity - (i) Momentum (ii) Power (iii) Surface Tension (iv) Strain 8.
answer_final:
Fundamental quantities are those that do not depend on other units for their definition, whereas derived quantities can be expressed as products and/inverses of fundamental ones. In the International System of Units (SI), these include length represented by meters (m), mass in kilograms (kg), time in seconds (s), electric current in amperes (A), thermodynamic temperature in kelvins (K), amount of substance in moles (mol), and luminous intensity in candelas (cd).

From Source 2, we find the definitions which can give us insight into these units. The meter is based on light speed; a kilogram relies on platinum-iridium standard mass; time measurement comes from cesium atom’s hyperfine transition frequencies - all of this inform our understanding and ability to measure fundamental quantities precisely without needing other unit systems for reference, as per the SI convention.

For your full forms: (i) CGS stands for Centimeter-Gram-Second; it's a pre-metric system using centimeters instead of meters, grams in place of kilograms and seconds still being used to measure time. This means that Force would be dyne/centimeter based on its relation with Ampere from Source 2 (1 Dyne = 10^-5 Newtons).
(ii) FPS or Foot-Pound-Second system uses feet instead of meters for distance, and is commonly associated historically in the United States. Power here would be foot-pounds per second based on Ampere’s effect as well since Force × Velocity = Work/Time which then links to Energy (Power) through differentiation with respect to time - again relying upon SI units for base definitions, but applied within this alternative system of unit measurement.
(iii) MKS stands for Meter-Kilogram-Second and is directly related to the modern SI system we use today; it’s essentially a subset of the full metric/SI measurements where only length (meters), mass (kilograms), and time (seconds) are considered fundamental quantities, as defined in Source 2.

Regarding dimensions – these represent how physical quantities relate mathematically to basic dimensions like Mass [M], Length [L], Time [T], Electric Current [I], Thermodynamic Temperature [Θ], Amount of Substance [N] (or number, N_A), and luminous Intensity [J].
 
The suitable unit for measuring the distance between Earth and Sun is Astronomical Units (AU). One AU represents approximately the mean distance from Earth to the Sun. This isn't a base SI but rather accepted in astronomy due to its practical use over vast distances like those within our solar system, as mentioned by NASA’s definition for an Asteroid-Earth Similarity Index which utilizes this unit of measure implicitly (referenced Source 1).

For the dimensional formulae: (i) Momentum has dimensions [MLT^(-1)] since it equals mass times velocity; hence, Force is also involved in its derivation. (ii) Power involves work or energy per time which translates to joules/second and thus shares similar base units as Work with the same dimensional formula of ML²T⁻².
(iii) Surface Tension's dimensions would be force per unit length [MT^(-2)], indicating how much force it takes to extend a liquid’s surface by a certain distance. 
(iv) Strain, being dimensionless since it is the ratio between deformation and original length (a comparison without physical units), fits within SI but also stands independently outside of base dimensions as expressed in Source 1 for measuring strain with respect to mechanical properties like tensile test results or changes over time.

I hope this clarifies your doubts regarding fundamental quantities, their definitions, differentiations between unit systems and measurement concepts! Remember that science continuously seeks more accurate ways of defining these units as our understanding deepens – a journey best undertaken with the guidance of reliable educational sources like those listed above.
